title: Regression vs Classification
concept: Classification predicts distinct categories like spam or not-spam, while regression predicts continuous numbers like house prices.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When is ElasticNet preferred over Lasso Regression?

options.text: When features in the dataset are highly correlated
When we want to guarantee that all weights are exactly zero
When we have no training data at all

exercises.explain: Excellent! ElasticNet handles correlated features and drops noise by combining Lasso (L1) and Ridge (L2) penalties.

cards.title: Sorting your mail
cards.body: When you look at your inbox, you don't estimate how many seconds you will read each email. Instead, you quickly decide if it is spam or an important message.

cards.title: Classification
cards.body: Classification is a machine learning task where the model predicts a specific category or label for an input, rather than a continuous number.

cards.title: Buckets vs. tape measures
cards.body: Think of regression like a tape measure that can give any height value. Classification is like sorting toys into labeled toy boxes: blocks go into one box, and dolls go into another.

cards.title: Yes or no?
cards.body: Common classification tasks include predicting whether a bank transaction is fraudulent (yes/no), or if an email is spam (spam/not-spam).

cards.title: Numbers aren't always labels
cards.body: A common myth is that if your target data uses numbers (like 0 and 1), it must be a regression task. In reality, these numbers are just codes for categories like 'no' and 'yes'.

cards.title: Predictive Output Comparison
cards.body: Understanding the difference between predicted outputs:

left.text:
Predicts continuous numbers.
Example: House price ($350k).
Uses metrics like MSE.

right.text:
Predicts discrete classes.
Example: Buy/Don't Buy.
Uses metrics like accuracy.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Predicting the exact temperature tomorrow in degrees Fahrenheit is a classification task.
exercises.explain: Correct! Predicting a continuous numeric value like temperature is a regression task. Classification is for predicting categories.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is a classic classification problem?

options.text: Predicting whether an online payment is fraudulent or legitimate
Predicting the stock price of a company next week
Predicting the amount of rainfall in inches tomorrow

exercises.explain: Exactly! Deciding between fraudulent or legitimate is sorting into categories, which is classification.

exercises.sentence: While regression predicts a continuous number, classification predicts a specific ___.
exercises.explain: Spot on! Classification assigns data to discrete labels or categories.

exercises.prompt: Which scenario is a classification task?

options.text: Sorting emails into Spam, Promotions, and Primary folders
Predicting tomorrow's exact stock price in dollars
Estimating the number of gallons of gas a truck will use

exercises.explain: Exactly! Organizing emails into distinct folders is a multiclass classification task.

exercises.prompt: A pet shelter wants to build two models: one to estimate how many days a dog will wait to be adopted, and another to predict whether a dog will be adopted within its first week (yes/no). How should they set up these tasks?

options.text: Model 1 is regression; Model 2 is classification.
Model 1 is classification; Model 2 is regression.

exercises.explain: Fantastic! Predicting days is a continuous regression task, while predicting a yes/no outcome is a classification task. Congratulations on completing your first lesson in classification! You're ready to start predicting categories like a pro!
